source_file: Dietary iron attenuates Clostridioides difficile infection via modulation of intestinal immune response and gut microbiota.txt

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: High dietary iron inhibits C. difficile colonization and TcdB toxin production.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Figure1m-n showed significantly reduced bacterial loads in cecal contents and cecum, and decreased TcdB titers in cecal contents and feces in the CDI+Iron diet group compared to the CDI group.

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: High dietary iron reduces intestinal neutrophil recruitment and pro-inflammatory cytokine secretion.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Figure2a showed decreased percentage of neutrophils in colonic lamina propria; Figure2c-h showed reduced mRNA expression of Il6, Tnfa, Il36g, Cxcl1, S100a8, S100a9 and lower protein levels of CXCL1, IL-36G, TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β in the cecum of iron-treated CDI mice.

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: High dietary iron alters gut microbiota composition, enriching Enterobacteriaceae (Escherichia-Shigella) and shifting microbial diversity.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Figure3j-o showed PCA/PCoA separation, reduced Shannon/Ace indices, and increased relative abundance of Enterobacteriaceae and Escherichia-Shigella at family and genus levels in the CDI+Iron diet group.

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Gut microbiota from iron-treated mice (FMT) alleviates CDI severity.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Figure3b-i showed that FMT from high-iron diet donors reduced DAI, histological scores, C. difficile loads, and IL-1β/TNF-α levels in recipient CDI mice.

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: E. coli AVS0501 (isolated from high-iron fed mice) protects against CDI by reducing C. difficile colonization, toxin production, neutrophil recruitment, and inflammation.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Figure4b-k showed E. coli AVS0501 treatment improved body weight and DAI, decreased C. difficile loads and TcdB, ameliorated histopathology, reduced colonic neutrophil percentages, and lowered IL-1β levels.

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Reducing intestinal L-proline levels decreases host susceptibility to CDI.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Figure6a showed E. coli AVS0501 reduced proline in cecal contents; Figure6b-c showed proline supplementation increased C. difficile growth and TcdB in vitro; Figure6e-n showed proline-free diet alleviated clinical symptoms, reduced bacterial loads, TcdB, neutrophil recruitment, and pro-inflammatory cytokines.

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Increasing intestinal TUDCA levels suppresses C. difficile colonization and toxin production, and alleviates CDI.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Figure7a-b showed TUDCA reduced C. difficile growth and TcdB in vitro; Figure7c-l showed TUDCA gavage improved body weight, DAI, decreased bacterial loads, TcdB, histological damage, neutrophil infiltration, and CXCL1/IL-1β levels.

key_findings: High dietary iron (400 mg/kg ferrous sulfate) attenuates primary and recurrent CDI in mice.
High dietary iron decreases C. difficile colonization and toxin production.
High dietary iron reduces neutrophil infiltration and inflammatory cytokine secretion in the colon.
High dietary iron reshapes gut microbiota, enriching Enterobacteriaceae, particularly Escherichia-Shigella.
E. coli AVS0501, identified from high-iron fed mice, exhibits prophylactic and therapeutic effects against CDI.
High dietary iron reduces intestinal L-proline levels and increases TUDCA levels in CDI mice.
L-proline promotes C. difficile growth and toxin production; proline-free diet significantly reduces CDI severity.
TUDCA inhibits C. difficile colonization and toxin production; TUDCA treatment alleviates CDI.
Fecal microbiota transplantation from high-iron fed mice partially protects against CDI.
relevance_to_research_topic: This paper directly addresses the research topic by investigating the effects of a high-iron diet on intestinal immune response, gut microbiota, and metabolism in a mouse model of C. difficile infection. It demonstrates that high dietary iron alleviates CDI by modulating host neutrophil recruitment, altering gut microbial composition (particularly enriching E. coli AVS0501), and regulating key metabolites (L-proline and TUDCA). The study elucidates the 'diet-gut microbiota-host' interplay in CDI susceptibility and severity, providing mechanistic insights into how dietary iron influences disease outcomes through specific bacterial strains and metabolites. These findings align with the user's goal of understanding the role of specific microbiota and metabolites in CDI.
